Hundreds of trees were uprooted or damaged.  Widespread damage also occurred to boats, docks, and boat lifts on Otter Tail Lake.  Wave erosion also occurred on beaches.  One lake resident said the storm produced the highest storm lake surge since 1948.  A metal patio chair was thrown through a ground floor window at one cabin.  Highway 78 was closed for a time as fallen trees made it impassable.  An estimated $2.9 million was spent by the county for cleanup and debris removal.  This figure did not include damage or cleanup expenses at residential or business properties.
A brief touchdown occurred near Pomme De Terre River.  A few trees were knocked over by winds estimated to be around 100 mph.
A funnel cloud was seen dipping down from the parent wall cloud.

Large trees were uprooted and a roof was torn off a house.  The top was torn off a wooden grain elevator in Ottertail and a nearby grain bin was bent inward.  A gas pump was blown over at a convenience store, causing a fire.  The tornado also tore the roof off a business in Ottertail and destroyed its sign.  A delivery truck was lifted off the ground and thrown 20 feet onto a nearby car.
Numerous trees were broken or uprooted in and around Perham.  One tree fell on a car.  Tree damage also occurred along U.S. Highway 10.
Heavy rain caused widespread street flooding in Lakefield.
Heavy rain caused flooding of numerous streets, stranding several vehicles.
Heavy rain caused a mudslide on Jackson County Road 23, with water and mud up to three feet deep.
Heavy rains of 3 to 5 inches fell in and around the city of Wheaton causing flash flooding. Many roads had water running over them with several of them closed.
Large hail covered the ground.
A wall cloud with pendant funnel was observed for about 25 minutes.
Two large trees were snapped off by the strong wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system travelled from near Winnipeg, Manitoba, into northwest Minnesota.  A cold front trailing behind this system brought a period of very strong wind speeds to a portion of the Devils Lake region and the northern Red River Valley.  The highest measured wind gusts were pretty uniform, around 60 mph.  Some roof damage was reported.

A low pressure system travelled from near Winnipeg, Manitoba, into northwest Minnesota.  A cold front trailing behind this system brought a period of very strong wind speeds to a portion of the Devils Lake region and the northern Red River Valley.  The highest measured wind gusts were pretty uniform, around 60 mph.  Some roof damage was reported.

A low pressure system travelled from near Winnipeg, Manitoba, into northwest Minnesota.  A cold front trailing behind this system brought a period of very strong wind speeds to a portion of the Devils Lake region and the northern Red River Valley.  The highest measured wind gusts were pretty uniform, around 60 mph.  Some roof damage was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered trees were downed.
Half of the roof of a barn was ripped off on Jewell Road in Belle Prairie Township, Section 14. A 2x4 from the roof was found lodged in a nearby shed. The time was estimated.
A storm chaser spotted a tornado near the intersection of Interstate 94 and Highway 2.
A storm chaser spotted a tornado.
A trained spotter saw a tornado briefly touch down in an open field. It kicked up dirt before lifting a short time later.
A trained spotter reported a funnel cloud.
Law enforcement reported numerous funnel clouds in and around Appleton.  One particular sighting was along Highway 119 about 4.5 miles north of town.
Law enforcement reported a funnel cloud at the intersection of county roads 4 and 59.
Law enforcement reported a funnel cloud.
A large barn was destroyed on a farm near the intersection of Highway 1 and 85th Avenue in Leon Township, Section 16. A building next to the barn was damaged by flying debris. The time was estimated.

A citizen on Duckwood Trail recorded a wind gust of 59 MPH. A tree on the property was downed onto a deck.  More than 50 trees were downed across the city, mainly south of Valley Creek Road and west of Woodbury Drive. The Colby Lake development was hardest hit. One home lost a corner of its roof and yard objects were thrown around.
Several trees were downed.  One downed tree blocked a road.
Several citizens reported funnel clouds in the area.
Several citizens reported funnel clouds in the area.
Law enforcement reported seeing a funnel cloud reach half way to the ground, then lift back into the clouds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storm spotters estimated wind gusts of 60 mph.
Storm spotters estimated wind gusts of 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was downed along Chestnut Street.
Large branches were downed by high winds that continued for at least 15 minutes.
60 to 70 mph (estimated) wind speeds occurred with a lot of tree damage.
A 108 mph wind gust was measured by the Crookston AWOS.  Several airport hangers sustained roof damage.
The roof was torn off a barn and two steel grain bins were blown down.
The tornado formed on the south edge of a downburst wind core.  The tornado was observed in a field just south of U.S. Highway 2 at Marcoux Corner.
Trees up to 18 inches in diameter were downed.
Several trees were downed.
A car dealership suffered significant hail damage losses.  Widespread roof and siding damage was also reported.
Hail began to fall at the size of walnuts and gradually decreased to the size of dimes 25 minutes later.
Two trees were blown down. Branches were blown down as well.
Power lines and trees were blown down in the Kabetogama and Ash River Trail areas. A 420 foot tower was blown over.
Trees were blown down, causing power outages.

Strong thunderstorm winds hit the Little Fork area. On County Road 73 a mobile home was overturned, large trees were blown down, breaking windows and causing other structural damage. Also, a pole barn collapsed.  On County Road 8, a barn collapsed, two sheds were blown into the woods, and over 100 trees were uprooted.
The strong winds damaged boats, docks, and boat lifts around Rush Lake.  Many trees were also blown down.

A work shed was ripped to pieces and another shed was blown away.  Significant damage also occurred to the roof and a wall of the home.  A solid steel weather vane was bent and many trees were blown down.
Highway 72 was closed near the southwest corner of Otter Tail Lake, as many trees were blown down onto the road.
A barn was blown over and a large metal bale feeder was tossed across the farmyard.
Uprooted trees broke windows and damaged the siding on a house.  Two trampolines were thrown into nearby trees.
A barn was pushed off its foundation and a horse was injured.
A metal storage shed was blown apart and lifted into some nearby power lines.
Trees were uprooted by the strong wind and 2 fell on a house.  Trees also fell on power lines and blocked city streets.

Downburst winds measured as high as 85 MPH (2 miles northwest of Swan Lake at 8:33 AM EDT) caused considerable damage in a 2 mile wide swath stretching from Highway 59 to the Pope County line in northeast Stevens County. The high wind damage path that started at Highway 59 was found between the following locations: 1 mile south of the Grant/Stevens county line and the County Road 20 intersection.  The path continued in an east-southeast direction toward the Pope County border and crossed the county line between the following locations: 1 mile north of County Road 20 and 150th Street.  A survey of the path revealed the following damage: 5 power poles were downed on the east side of Highway 59 along with significant tree damage nearby; significant tree and corn damage along County Road 20 from Highway 59 to the Pope County line.  See the Pope County entry for damage reports east of the county line.

County Road C was impassable due to flooding near the intersection of 35E in Little Canada.  An underpass on Highway 36 in Maplewood was also flooded. A spotter near the intersection of Interstate 94 and McKnight Road in Maplewood recorded 0.80 inches of rain in 15 minutes. A spotter in St. Paul recorded 1.25 inches of rain in 30 minutes.
A small tree was downed.
A 6 inch diameter tree was downed.
A 121-year-old barn was blown down along Highway 72 in Morristown Township, Section 14.
Dime to nickel sized hail fell.
Trees were blown down, including a 50 foot pine.
Trees and power lines were blown down.
Hundreds of electric customers were without power as high winds downed power lines and lightning hit transformers.
About 50 homes lost power as trees fell on power lines.
Quarter sized hail was reported at St. Cloud State University. Dime sized hail fell at 12th Street near 25th Avenue. Small branches were downed.
A wind gust of 58 MPH was recorded at the Willmar airport.
A power line and tree branches were downed.

Significant flooding was reported in the North Fork Zumbro River watershed during the evening of the 7th after 3 to 6 inches of rain fell. A total of 5.47 inches of rain was recorded in Wanamingo in 24 hours, 2.8 inches of which fell between 9:30 and 10:30 PM.  An unnamed creek which flows into the North Fork Zumbro River rose 20 feet above its normal 1 foot level and rushed over Highway 1 about 1 mile south of the Highway 30 intersection in Wanamingo Township, about 4 miles west of Wanamingo. By 10:30 PM the creek completed covered the road in rushing water up to 2.5 feet deep and shortly thereafter two separate vehicles were swept off the roadway and were washed down the creek.  One vehicle, a pickup truck, was found in a nearby ditch with a smashed windshield and badly damaged hood.  The body of the driver, a 47-year-old man, was found three quarters of a mile downstream under farm equipment nearly a day and a half later when the water finally subsided. The man, who lived in Hayfield, was on his way to work in Cannon Falls.  The other vehicle that was washed off the road and into the creek was a sedan. The driver, a 50-year-old Kenyon woman who was on her way home from work, exited the car through an open window after traveling about ½ mile downstream. She floated on her back down the creek until lightning illuminated a nearby fencepost which she grabbed hold of until rescuers arrived two hours later.  Incredibly she survived the ordeal with only bruises. The rushing creek was also responsible for washing out 200 feet of gradual slope along Highway 1 near the road. Scattered debris was also found in farm fields along the creek's banks for several miles.  In addition, an unnamed creek that flows into the North Fork Zumbro River about 2 miles east of Wanamingo inundated the Shades of Sherwood campground around 10:30 PM.
Medium sized trees were downed.

Trees up to 5 inches in diameter were downed around Lake of the Isles and Lake Calhoun. A large oak tree was downed in south Minneapolis.  A trained spotter recorded a wind gust of 65 MPH on an anemometer.
Quarter sized hail fell at North Memorial Hospital.

Several roads were covered by water including 100th Avenue and 45th Avenue just south of Highway 27.   Rainfall amounts of 3.5 to 4.5 inches were reported in the Isle, Onamia, and Wahkon areas.  Most of the rain fell in one hour.
Dime sized hail fell at the intersection of Highway 41 and County Road 11.
Trees and a few power lines were downed.
A total of 3.25 inches of rain was recorded in 30 minutes 5 miles south of Fort Ripley.
Dime to nickel sized hail fell at the intersection of Cliff Road and Nicollet.
The roof of a shed was blown off at a farm on 160th Street.
Tree branches up to 4 inches in diameter were downed.
Trees were downed.
An outbuilding at a farm was destroyed by a lightning-related fire on 110th Street in Section 36 of Iosco Township. The time was estimated.

A bolt of lightning struck the fascia of a home on 368th Avenue and came out through the siding. Three people inside the structure felt tingling immediately after the bolt hit, but were uninjured.  The time was estimated.
Penny sized hail fell near the Waseca and Steele County border east of Waseca.
An 18 inch diameter tree was downed onto Highway 14 in Meridan Township.

A machine shed and bin collapsed onto a combine at a farm on Highway 20 in Fairfield Township, Section 33. The debris from the shed and bin were thrown several yards. A farmhouse suffered a broken window and a hole in the roof from flying debris. At the property next door a machine shed had the roof and a door ripped off. Evergreens at a church in Fairfield were downed.
Several trees up to 7 inches in diameter were downed across the western portion of the county including in the communities of Winnebago and Blue Earth.
Nickel to quarter sized hail fell.
A few trees were downed. A large branch was downed onto a power line.
A citizen reported a funnel cloud between Clear Lake and Becker.  The time was estimated.
Law enforcement spotted a funnel cloud.
A cottonwood tree was felled onto a ride at the fairgrounds. The time was estimated.
A tree was downed.
Minor street flooding was reported in St. Louis Park and Deephaven.
Trees were downed in a 6 block area of town.
Law enforcement reported several cars stalled in flood waters.
Penny sized hail fell near Highview School.
A tree was downed across County Road 7 about one mile north of County Road 5 just north of Spencer Brook.
Penny sized hail fell in Otsego Township.
A few trees and many branches were downed.
Trees and power lines were downed in Columbia Heights.  Power outages were reported in Coon Rapids and Fridley.
Quarter sized hail fell near the intersection of Interstate 35E and County Road E.
Trees and power lines were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storm spotters reported wind gusts of 60 mph, along with hail the size of nickels.

With the ground already saturated from heavy rain the previous evening, another round of heavy rain caused flash flooding.  The resultant high water forced the closure of Highway 63 in Zumbro Falls.  In addition, a storm spotter in Zumbro Falls reported a 24 hour rainfall total of 5.95 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch of rain fell in less than 30 minutes, causing street flooding in Park Rapids.
The wind gust was measured by an NDAWN wind sensor.
Two trees were uprooted and many large branches were blown down.

Trees and power lines were downed in Woodbury, Cottage Grove, Lake Elmo, St. Paul Park, Afton, Lakeland, and Marine on the St. Croix, and Stillwater.  In Cottage Grove, one tree was blown down onto a house, and lightning started a shed fire.  In Stillwater a garage and pickup truck were damaged by a downed tree. A tree house was also damaged when the large branch it was supported by was ripped from the tree.
Branches up to 5 inches in diameter were downed on Grand Avenue near the intersection of Snelling Avenue and along Snelling Avenue near the intersection of Summit Avenue.
Dime sized hail was reported at Big Lake. The time was estimated.
A storm chaser witnessed a tornado briefly touch down near Minneopa State Park. Two other storm chasers also confirmed the touchdown from different vantage points.
Penny sized hail fell at Clear Lake.
Trees were downed onto yards in addition to a car on Nevada Avenue and a van on 18th Avenue.
A downed tree smashed a grain wagon.
Large branches were downed onto power lines.
A cattle shed lost most of its roof at a farm along Highway 12 about 1 mile west of the Highway 5 intersection in Shible Township, Section 15. A machine shed was nearly demolished nearby.

The roof of three sheds and a hog barn were blown off at a farm on the south side of Lake Oliver near 20th Street NW in Hegbert Township, Section 35. A two by four was blown into the side of a farmhouse and windows were broken by flying debris.  Trees were also downed on the property.
A tree was downed.
A few trees and power lines were downed.
Eleven power poles were snapped along Manning Avenue (County Road 15) south of County Road 10 near Oak-Land Junior High School.
Dime to quarter sized hail fell near the intersection of County Road 44 and Highway 13.
The fire department reported a wind gust of 60 MPH.
Between 15 and 20 sailboats anchored to buoys were overturned on Lake Calhoun. Tree limbs up to 5 inches in diameter were downed. A wooden fence was blown over.
Dime sized hail fell at the intersection of Interstate 94 and McKnight Road.
A small tree was downed.
A tree was downed.
Flooding was reported on the Highway 19 underpass at Interstate 35.
At least 12 trees up to 50 feet tall and 20 inches in diameter were downed in the Norcrest Avenue area.
Two trees were downed.  One tree fell onto a garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Red River in Fargo/Moorhead rose to a crest around 28.19 feet on June 18th.  Moderate flooding occurs at Fargo/Moorhead when the Red River reaches a height of 27 feet.  This was the second highest summer crest on record.  The highest summer crest of 33.26 feet occurred on July 4, 1975.  The flooding Red River affected recreational paths, golf courses, and bridges.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were downed.
Trees were downed. The time was estimated.
Lightning struck a home on Timberline Trail.  The northwest corner of the home caught fire and sheetrock fell down on a person lying in bed.
Quarter sized hail fell near Highway 96 and Otter Lake Road.
The city public works department reported penny sized hail at the corner of Lincolntown Avenue and Long Lake Road.
Walnut sized hail fell for 10 minutes in Leon Township, Section 8.

Along 430th Street in Holden Township, Section 23, a large commodity shed was destroyed after 5 support beams were ripped from the structure. One of the beams was thrown 75 feet and the roof of the structure fell onto a tractor.  Doors on two free standing barns were also damaged. In addition, a very large Maple tree that was estimated to be over 100 years old was felled.
A newspaper published a picture of hail that fell in Kenyon that looked to be at least 1.25 inches in diameter.
About 3 miles south of Nerstrand, 210th St. E. was under water.  Water was also flowing over secondary roads in Wheeling, Walcott, and Richland townships.
Penny sized hail fell at the intersection of County Road 2 and Interstate 35.
Dozens of trees were blown down and power was lost.  One tree fell on a van, crushing it.
A brief touchdown was reported.
Large tree branches were blown down.
Four inch tree branches were downed.
A county employee reported a funnel cloud near the intersection of county roads 25 and 56. The public reported a funnel cloud near the intersection of county roads 114 and 27.
A citizen measured a wind gust of 71 MPH with an anemometer.
Moderate to large trees were downed from Forest Lake to Hugo to Newport to St. Paul Park.
A 58 MPH wind gust was recorded at Minneapolis/St. Paul International Airport.
Golf ball sized hail fell near the intersection of Interstate 35E and Maryland Avenue.
Power lines were downed.

Over 400 trees up to 3 feet in diameter and many power lines were downed across the city by winds estimated up to 70 MPH. Many trees blocked roads and a handful of trees fell onto buildings and cars. The damage was so widespread that a state of emergency was put in place shortly after the storm ended until streets could be cleared.
Several power lines and trees up to 2 feet in diameter were downed. A few downed trees damaged homes. The hardest hit area was just north of County Road 47 and south of Highway 55.
Eighteen trees and a handful of power lines were downed. One tree fell onto a construction site in the 2900 block of Sumter, causing minor damage.
Eight trees up to 12 inches in diameter and numerous branches were downed.

Roads were covered in water and drainage systems were overflowing in Minneapolis. The intersection of 58th Avenue and Emmerson was flooded up to curb level. Flooding was also reported in Minneapolis at the intersection of Interstate 94 and Highway 55 and on Broadway near West River Road. In Robbinsdale a car became stalled in flood waters at the intersection of County Road 81 and 40th Street.  Ponding of water was reported on roads in Osseo.  High water damaged the southbound bridge approach at Highway 169 at Interstate 694 in Maple Grove.
Three inch branches were downed.
Nickel sized hail was reported south of Winnebago. One half inch diameter hail fell in Winnebago.
Roof damage was reported near the intersection of Annapolis and Roberts Street.
An 8 inch diameter tree was downed.
Several roads were flooded, including the intersection of 12th Street and 25th Avenue.
Small branches and many leaves were downed by the hail that fell 1 mile north of the St. Cloud Regional Airport.
The top half of a chicken barn was blown off along 17th Avenue in Linden Township.
A large dump truck was forced off the road by high winds.
A 20-year-old birch tree was downed.

A tree and several branches were downed onto roads and yards in town.  The most concentrated area of damage was near 3rd Street and 2nd Avenue, including a tree that fell on a vacant house. A construction site about 5 miles southwest of Madison suffered building damage.  Grain bins were also damaged in the area.
Law enforcement officers reported downed trees.
Two large trees up to 1 meter in diameter were broken about ½ mile west of the intersection of 90th and France Ave.
Quarter sized hail covered the ground and damaged crops.
Quarter sized hail dented cars and broke windows.
Several trees were downed.
Two trees up to 10 inches in diameter were downed near the intersection of Douglas Drive and Rockford Road. One tree fell onto a car.

A citizen videotaped a tornado that touched down just south of the Pine County line near the intersection of River Road and 540th Street in a heavily wooded area. The tornado downed some trees and pushed other trees over at an angle in this location before crossing the border into Pine County.
A storm chaser reported a funnel cloud just north of Sedan.
The quantity of hail that fell was so large that it had to be shoveled off decks and sidewalks and scooped out of gutters.
The Sheriff's department stated that several motorists reported seeing a funnel cloud.
Several spotters reported a funnel cloud near Interstate 94 and Keats Avenue.

A trained spotter downtown recorded a 62 MPH wind gust.  A gust of 56 MPH was recorded at Watertown-Mayer High School just northwest of town. Large tree limbs were downed onto Westminster Avenue south of Madison Street.  Another large limb was downed onto vehicles on Angel Avenue SW.  The roof of a shed was blown off along Tacoma Avenue in Hollywood Township, three miles southwest of Watertown.
A trained spotter recorded a wind gust of 66 MPH. Numerous trees were downed.

At least 25 large trees were downed across Le Sueur. One tree fell onto a home at Nichols Court and caused considerable damage to the roof and an upstairs bedroom.  Another tree blocked County Road 112.
Strong thunderstorm winds took down trees and phone lines in the Camp 5 Township, including the Elephant Lake and Ash River areas.
Trees were blown down and power was lost.
Trees were blown down and a boat lift was thrown 50 feet.
The end walls were blown out of a pole shed.  Numerous large trees were snapped off or uprooted.
A MNDOT RWIS site measured the 68 mph wind gust.
Trees were blown down and some shingle damage was reported.
A MNDOT RWIS site measured the 58 mph wind gust.
A MNDOT RWIS site measured the 58 mph wind gust.
A brief tornado touchdown was reported east of Fertile.
Several large trees were blown down by the strong wind.  Shingles were torn off two homes.
The roof was blown off a house.
Two trees up to 10 inches in diameter were downed.  A power line was downed onto Elm Avenue.
Minor tree branch and cars damage was reported.
A trained spotter reported a funnel cloud 2 miles north of Wanda or about 2 miles southeast of Wabasso.

A tree was downed in Victoria near the intersection of Highway 7 and Smithtown Road. A tree was downed near South Shore Drive in Chanhassen. A tree fell onto a power line near Mellgren Lane in Chaska.
Several trees were downed across the county.  Communities that sustained tree damage included Anoka, Coon Rapids, Fridley, and Columbia Heights.
Numerous trees were downed.

Large trees were downed in a corridor stretching from the southern edge of Lake Marion to the southern half of Lakeville.  A few trees fell onto homes, cars, and power lines. City officials reported the loss of 50 boulevard trees and damage to limbs on hundreds of others in town. Seventeen large trees were lost in Aronson and Antlers Park and at the Lakeville Area Arts Center. A 10-foot-tall white fence was blown down.

Numerous trees were downed onto homes, streets, and yards. Several homes sustained significant roof damage and broken windows. A fallen tree punched a hole in the roof and a branch stuck through into an upstairs bedroom at a home on Division Street South.  A person sleeping in a bed below the hole was awakened when insulation fell on them but was otherwise unharmed. A trampoline in the yard was thrown with such force that it bent a fence.  A town resident said the high winds lasted for nearly 15 minutes.
A tree in a marsh near the Minnesota River was downed onto a power line.
A tree and several branches were downed.
Dime sized hail was reported south and west of Benson.
Trees and power lines were downed near Benson.
Large branches were downed.
Penny sized hail fell near Lake Emily.
Trees were downed onto two mobile homes.
Trees and large branches were downed.
Numerous trees were downed.
Large branches and signs were downed.

Law enforcement officers reported a wind gust measured at 65 MPH. Trees up to 2 feet in diameter and power lines were downed.  A few downed trees damaged homes and cars, and several large branches littered streets. A dugout at the New Prague High School softball field was lifted up and deposited on a nearby shed. An awning on a building on East Main Street was torn away.

A 4 mile long path of concentrated wind damage was discovered across Cedar Lake Township. The path of damage started near the intersection of Highway 13 and 270th Street East and continued to the east northeast across Cedar Lake and ended near the intersection of highways 85 and 56.  Several trees and power lines were downed along the path of the high winds. Homes in this damage path sustained shingle and siding damage. One outbuilding near the intersection of Highway 2 and Vergus Avenue had its roof partially removed. Wood and insulation from the roof was blown up to 1500 feet away from the structure. A garage door on a large storage building was damaged nearby.  Many trees were felled around Cedar Lake. On Cedar Lake Drive a pontoon boat was raised off its lift and thrown upside down onto the shoreline.
A funnel cloud was reported via HAM radio about 2 miles northeast of Godahl near the intersection of County Road 4 and 132.
A 3 inch diameter tree limbs were downed at the intersection of Cedar Avenue and 157th Street.
A tree was downed onto power lines.

Several boats were capsized and docks were destroyed by waves created by high winds at a resort on Lake Washington.  Trees were also downed in the vicinity. The framing of a new house under construction along the shore of the lake was smashed by a downed tree.
A tree was downed onto a car. A handful of other trees and power lines were downed across town.

Several trees and power lines were downed. A truck was damaged on First Avenue SW by a downed tree. A home on First Avenue NE was struck by a pine tree that was snapped off at its base. A city vehicle shed was blown apart on the north side of town and the roof was deposited 100 yards away. Several downed trees blocked South Lakeshore Drive. Several pontoon boats and the lifts they were attached to were blown into Lake Minnewaska.
A few trees were downed.
A trained spotter reported via HAM Radio that they witnessed a funnel cloud from a vantage point 7 miles south of Sauk Center.
Trees were blown down at Riley's Supper Club.
The strong winds tore the trim off a building and knocked over several large trees.
2.5 inches of rain fell in an hour, flooding city streets.

Several trees were downed on the south and east side of Third Lake. The hardest hit area was near the intersection of North Shore Trail (County Road 2) and Jeffrey Avenue.  A number of downed trees damaged homes and blocked roads. Blown in windows were also reported.  In addition, downed trees were reported at Castlewood Golf Course.  A large tree branch was also downed onto County Road 47 near Highway 61.  At the Forest Lake Airport several doors were torn from a hangar and one third of the roof of another hangar was blown off.  Six planes housed in these hangars were damaged.
Power lines were downed.
Several large trees over 1 foot in diameter were downed.
A small tree was downed onto Highway 61.

A tornado that touched down northwest of Madelia was witnessed by numerous spotters, law enforcement officials, and storm chasers.  It first touched down on farmland in Riverdale Township, Section 13, along County Road 3. It slightly damaged a nearby farmhouse and a cornfield along 790th Avenue about ½ mile north of County Road 3. The tornado crossed into Madelia Township where it downed a few trees and branches before dissipating.
Four inch tree branches were downed onto Interstate 94.

A dirt berm had to be constructed on Highway 28-29 to stop flood waters from flowing across the highway near the fairgrounds on the northwest side of town. Low lying property and a few roads nearby were flooded. An estimated 2.5 inches of rain fell in 30 minutes in Glenwood.
Dime sized hail fell at the Interstate 94 exit to Osakis.
A few trees were downed.
Pea to quarter sized hail fell for 15 minutes.
Branches were downed.
Large tree branches were blown down and power lines were knocked down.  Scattered power outages were also reported.
Street flooding was reported after two inches of rain fell in less than an hour.
Intermittent tornado touchdowns occurred to the Becker county line about 4 miles northeast of Rollag.  The tornado continued into Becker county.
This tornado originated about 3 miles northeast of Rollag in Clay county.  Numerous trees were knocked down along the tornado track.  Peak winds were estimated at 90 mph.

The tornado flattened an older barn and uprooted several trees.  Two people narrowly escaped the tornado as it hit the barn.  The landowner reported that another tornado had hit another barn on same site back in 1933.  Peak winds were estimated at 130 mph.
A MNDOT RWIS sensor along U.S. Highway 10 measured the wind gust.
6 to 8 inch diameter tree limbs were ripped off.
A large branch was torn off a tree.
A MNDOT RWIS sensor along U.S. Highway 10 measured the wind gust.
The tornado knocked down numerous trees along county road 24.  Estimated peak winds were 90 mph.  The tornado continued into Beltrami county.
The tornado originated 3 miles east-northeast of Leonard in Clearwater county.  Many large trees were knocked over along county road 24.  Estimated peak wind speeds were 90 mph.
The tornado tracked through trees and crossed scenic highway 39 south of Blackduck.  Many large trees were knocked down along the damage path.  Peak wind speeds were estimated at 100 mph.
Fields were flooded near highway 175 and county road 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall toward the end of June pushed the Red River at Wahpeton/Breckenridge back above its moderate flood stage of 12 feet.  The crest for this event didn't occur until early July.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado originated 4 miles east-northeast of Osage in Becker county.  The tornado tracked to near Portage Lake and knocked down several trees.  Peak winds were estimated at 100 mph.
Large branches were blown down.  A wind gust of 51 mph was measured at the Park Rapids airport at 346 pm CDT.
4 inch diameter branches were blown down.
Trees were downed.

Trees and power lines were downed along Orange Avenue near White Bear Avenue. A fence and swing set were damaged by the felled trees. At least one tree was also downed onto a vehicle.  A fire was also sparked by downed wires.

Trees were uprooted and 4 homes and a deck were damaged along the east shore of Lake Stella along County Road 14.  One man was trapped in a porch when a tree fell on it and had to break a window to escape.  A boat lift and attached boat were blown over a dock and rolled a few feet along the Lake Stella shoreline. Six foot waves were reported on Lake Stella and water lapped up to County Road 14. In addition, power lines were downed over 650th Avenue near Casey Lake and along 670th Avenue near County Road 9.

Five power poles were snapped off, 13 light poles were downed, and a number of trees were uprooted in Nicollet.  One large tree branch fell onto a car on Third Street. Tree and power line damage was also reported in Courtland and St. Peter. In Lafayette, 10 trees were damaged and 2x6 wooden bleachers at a ball field were snapped and pushed over on their sides. Seven miles east of Lafayette in New Sweden Township, a 25 foot diameter Ash tree was broken off at its base and thrown 150 feet. Several trees in a nearby grove were twisted and broken about 25 feet up in the canopy. In addition, a circa 1930s steel bin with a 1500 bushel capacity was twisted up and rolled at least 200 feet.
Hail up to the size of quarters fell on Highway 24. A car was dented by the hail.

A trained spotter recorded a wind gust of 61 MPH.  A 12 inch diameter tree was snapped in half. Another tree toppled onto a car near the Fifth Avenue bridge. The tree smashed the windshield and rear window and also dented the roof and hood.
At least two trees and power lines were downed.  One felled tree blocked Second Street Northeast and the top of the tree landed on a vehicle. A flag pole was also knocked down.

A 2 mile wide swath of downburst wind damage continued from Stevens County into northwest Pope County.  The damage path crossed the county line between the following locations: 1 mile north of County Road 32 and 150th Street.  The path continued in an east-southeast direction and was last noted stretching from Lowry to 1 mile north of Starbuck. A survey of the path revealed the following damage: significant tree and corn damage along County Road 26; damage to the roof of a home along County Road 26 about 3 miles east of the county line from a fallen 2 foot diameter tree branch; a flattened 100-year-old barn (3 cattle killed inside), a damaged farm house, and demolished cement silo 2 miles south of Farwell; a lost machine shed and partial destruction of a dairy barn about 2.5 miles south of Farwell; significant tree damage in Lowry (in the vicinity of where a wind gust of 89 MPH was measured by a home weather station) including the roof of a home that was damaged by a felled tree; destruction of a large garage on the southeast side of Lowry along County Road 28; a caved-in metal grain bin and a heavily damaged barn on Highway 114 three miles south of Lowry; significant damage to a homestead near the intersection of Highway 114 and 190th Street about 1 mile north of Starbuck; numerous downed trees 3 miles north of Starbuck including one which damaged a car.  Minor tree damage was noted east of Highway 114 as well, but the high winds that caused the damage dissipated shortly after passing this location.  One observer 3 miles north of Starbuck noted that the high winds continued off and on for nearly 30 minutes, coming in 6 distinct "waves".

The Red River at Wahpeton-Breckenridge rose over its moderate flood stage of 12 feet around 4 pm CDT on June 30, 2005.  The river crested around 12.56 feet on July 1st, around 2 pm CDT.  The Red River then fell quickly, back below its moderate flood stage around noon CDT on the 3rd.

Up to 6 inches of rain fell in the North Fork Zumbro River watershed on the evening of the 7th and widespread flooding resulted. The Sheriff's department reported that flood waters from the North Fork Zumbro River forced the closure of Highway 58 just north of Zumbrota at Covered Bridge Park around 3 AM on the 8th. The highway remained closed for much of the day. Covered Bridge Park along the river sustained $20,000 damage to electrical equipment in the campground area, and a softball tournament had to be cancelled at the park after the flood damaged ball fields. The wastewater treatment plant in Wanamingo had to be evacuated shortly after midnight after the Zumbro River became higher than the water discharge level and began to flood back into the plant. A total of 4.48 inches of rain was recorded at the plant in 24 hours. Local officials had to sandbag low lying areas of town. In addition, the Shades of Sherwood campground, about 2 miles northeast of Wanamingo, was severely flooded when the North Fork Zumbro River and an unnamed tributary overflowed during the early morning hours. The campground sustained around $25,000 in damage.  Approximately 30 camper trailers suffered heavy damage or were completely destroyed by flood waters. Total damage to personal property at the campground was estimated at more than $500,000. Various county roads near the river were impassible for much of the day, including County Road 1 about 4 miles west of Wanamingo between Highway 30 and Highway 60, Highway 57 just north of Wanamingo, and 420th Street about 5 miles northwest of Zumbrota. Several shoulders on roads passing the North Fork Zumbro River had to be repaired after being washed out.  About 3 miles west of Cannon Falls, the Little Cannon River overflowed into a yard along Oxford Mill Road. In Kenyon, a country club golf course was flooded by the North Fork Zumbro River.

High temperatures at Minneapolis-St. Paul International Airport remained at or above 90 degrees for 9 consecutive days between July 9th and 17th.  This extended period of hot weather set a record for the 3rd longest streak of at or above 90 degree highs since 1891 in the Twin Cities.  On July 12th, a laborer putting up a fence in Arden Hills in Ramsey County suffered severe heatstroke.  He collapsed at the work site and was rushed to a local hospital.  His body temperature reached 108.8 degrees, but miraculously he survived after receiving intensive medical attention. He awoke from a medically-induced sedation 24 hours after falling ill and made a full recovery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Red River at East Grand Forks (MN) rose to a crest around 40.36 feet on June 18th.  Moderate flooding occurs at East Grand Forks when the Red River reaches a height of 40 feet.  This was the second highest summer crest on record.  The highest summer crest of 43.08 feet occurred on July 14, 1975.  East Grand Forks closed the 4th street northwest off-ramp from U.S. Highway 2.

The Red River at Wahpeton/Breckenridge had 3 crests above its moderate flood stage during the month of June.  These 3 occurred in near succession, one around 12.9 feet on June 9th, the second around 12.7 feet on June 12th, and the third around 13.1 feet on June 15th.  The Bois de Sioux River spilled over its banks south of Breckenridge, flooding several township roads.  The U.S. Highway 75 underpass north of Kent was closed due to high water.

Two people in the second cabin sustained bumps and bruises when a large tree fell on top of the bedroom they were staying in. The tree fell right on top of the bed where they were sleeping moments before. They were standing nearby when the tree "split the ceiling open" above them. A nearby trailer home was also damaged by the tree that fell onto the unoccupied cabin.  One witness said that wind was blowing so hard that water was "flying off the lake." Power crews had to replace several electricity poles between East Jefferson Lake and German Lake that were damaged by downed trees.
Dime to nickle sized hail was reported.
Water flowed over highway 72 from Cormorant Creek and Battle River.
A sheriff's deputy reported trees bent over and blinding rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Two Rivers River at Hallock rose above its moderate flood stage of 806 feet MSL late in the month of June.  The actual crest occurred in early July.

Thunderstorm winds blew off the roof of an apartment building, and damaged buildings at a lumber yard and at a school bus yard. The winds also caused widespread tree damage, including several trees uprooted.

A torrnado briefly touched down and tracked eastward for about one-quarter of a mile across the north edge of a farmstead.  Numerous trees were snapped off or uprooted.  A 12x48 foot mobile home was flipped onto its top and destroyed.  The mobile home had been resting on its transport wheels in an east-west orientation and was not anchored.  It was flipped about 25 yards to the northeast and landed in a north-south orientation.  Peak winds were estimated at 110 mph.
Heavy rains caused Elm Creek to rise over its banks, flooding adjoining fields.

Trees and power lines were blown down in and around Aitkin. Trees were blown onto Highways 18, 65, and 169. There was also damage near Malmo. The damage area was 25 miles wide and 40 miles long across southern Aitkin County. 3500 households were without power, some for up to 4 days.

The tornado tore the doors off a steel building, flipped a camper trailer, and uprooted around 50 trees.  Around 30 power poles were damaged, causing $150,000 in damages to the local utility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season winter storm began as an area of surface low pressure near Colorado.  The low tracked to near Sioux Falls, SD, by tuesday evening (December 4th).  Then, the low lifted nearly straight north, reaching the Fargo, ND, area by mid-morning on the 5th.  The low continued to lift to the north, reaching Reynolds, ND, by late afternoon.  The low then began to shift east, passing near Bemidji by late evening on the 5th.  The storm system brought a variety of weather to the region.  Very strong northeast winds occurred while the surface low was in North Dakota, then winds turned more north-northwest as the low passed into Minnesota.  Temperatures were fairly warm initially, so the precipitation began as rain.  However, as temperatures dropped, the precipitation changed to freezing rain and then to heavy, wet snow.  The heaviest snow fell across northern Towner and northern Cavalier Counties, where Sarles reported 14 inches and Calvin and Munich reported 10 inches.  Lower amounts were reported to the south and east, with Leeds at 8 inches, Langdon at 7 inches, Walhalla at 6 inches, Starkweather at 5 inches, and Cavalier and Devils Lake at 4 inches.  The heavy, wet snow bogged down power lines and caused sporadic line breaks.

The strong northeast wind had a long trajectory over Lake of the Woods, which caused the most problems for the Warroad area.  High waves and water levels at the mouth of the Warroad River significantly damaged the Warroad public dock.  Roof shingles and several small storage sheds were also damaged in town.  A building under construction in Baudette was blown over.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads and basements were flooded across the county.  Streets were also flooded in Benson. Ditches and tributaries leading to the Chippewa River in Swift Falls overflowed. A road in Swift Falls was washed out and had to be rebuilt after the water receded.  A handful of other roads around Swift Falls were partially eroded by flood waters. In addition, several township roads in the northern and eastern portion of the county were under water for up to 24 hours, especially in Camp Falls Township. Swift Falls Park had to be evacuated after flooding was observed. Swift Falls Creek overflowed its banks around 12:30 AM CDT. Rainfall totals included 8.18 inches one mile southwest of Kerkhoven, 4.98 inches in downtown Benson and 3.59 inches at the Benson airport.

In Morrison County, roads were barricaded near the intersection of State Highway 25 and Highway 234.  In Benton County, creeks and rivers overran their banks with quite a few lowlands across the county water covered. Some road water covered. In Hennepin County, Minnehaha Creek flooded over its banks in South Minneapolis. Nearby roads including Minnehaha Parkway remained under water.  Street flooding was reported in many cities including Hopkins, Minnetonka, Deephaven, St. Anthony, Minneapolis, and  Bloomington. Many people reported water in their basements. St. Louis Park had significant street flooding in the following areas: Hwy 100 at 36th St., Louisiana at Walker, County Road 25 and Minnetonka Blvd. at Huntington, Minnetonka Blvd at Rhode Island. In Scott County, Credit River near Highway 13 overflowed its banks, and flooded a side street along the highway in front of the Fire Department. In Carver County, Market and 79th Street remained under water,and Prescott Court flooded. A storm water pond near Longacres Drive overflowed its banks. In Ramsey County, there was underpass and parking lot flooding across Roseville.  In New Brighton, underpass and parking lot flooding across town, and approximately 20 cars were totally or partially submerged in a parking lot of the Polynesian Village apartment complex in the 1400 block of 10th Street Northwest.

Several roads were flooded in and around Willmar. Rainfall totals included 8.30 inches three miles west of New London, 6.72 inches two miles southeast of Sunburg, 5.52 inches in New London, 3.15 inches at the Willmar wastewater treatment plant, and 3.01 inches at the Willmar airport.
A total of 4.50 inches of rain was recorded three miles south of Kerkhoven in the eastern portion of Chippewa County.

Flooding was widespread.  In Woodbury, there was backyard flooding on Duckwood Trail, two miles south of Interstate 94 near Radio Drive.  Several large ponds developed, with the largest pond encompassing 50 ft. by 150 ft. and up to 3.5 feet deep.  One foot of moving water in back yards and streets was near the east side of Eagle Valley Golf course in Woodbury, located South of Valley Creek Rd. on Cottage Grove Dr.  In Cottage Grove, one to two feet of water was reported on 80th Street between Jamaica and Hornell Ave. Waist deep water occurred at the bottom of the hill on Hardwood Ave, north of 80th St. Ten vehicles were floating at this location. A spotter measured 7.5 inches rain in Woodbury (near Lake Road and Radio Drive), while another Woodbury spotter (near Highland Circle and 90th), registered 7.04 inches total,  including 5.74 inches between 1900 and 2230 CST.  A storm chaser in Oakdale picked up 6.5 inches, and 5.62 inches fell in Stillwater.

A lightning strike hit a home in the 7900 Block of 71st Street. The bolt caused an immediate explosion, and injured two 50 year olds who owned the home. The individuals were transported to Regions Hospital, where they were listed in fair and stable condition on the 5th. The fire destroyed the residence and caused some damage to neighboring residences.

Lightning struck the gas main of a home on Bluebill Bay Road, sparking a four alarm fire. There was significant damage to the home, but no injuries.

Flooding was widespread across much of the county.  In Minneapolis, a section of East River Road (behind Elliot Hall on the University of Minnesota Campus) washed out.  Water rushing through intersection at 38th and Cedar was knee deep and pouring over automobile bumpers, and flooding was over curbs.  Flooding in yards in the 4100 block of 31st Avenue was up to 18 inches deep, where a rain gauge measured 4.25 inches 2130 CST.  Interstate 35W near Diamond Lake Road was closed. Minnehaha Creek overflowed its banks between Lakes Harriet and Nokomis.  In the southern Minneapolis suburbs, portions of Highway 494 were also closed during the evening hours. In Eden Prairie, there was damage to a culvert on West 78th Street at Nine Mile Creek. City repair crews repaired sink holes that developed in the westbound and eastbound traffic lanes after sandy soil beneath the pavement was washed away. Cars flooded at Highway 5 and Mitchell, and the exit ramp from 494 and Valley View Road required mud and sand removal. In Richfield, one foot of water gathered at the intersection of Portland and 70th St. In Bloomington, American Blvd between Nicollet Ave and Portland (0.25 miles SE of I-494 at Nicollet Ave), was flooded over the curbs, cars became disabled, and the road was impassible.  Some   people in St Louis Park were rescued from flooded vehicles. Rainfall totals included 6.2 inches in Minnetonka and 4.86 inches in South Minneapolis (Chowen and 40th), and 4.27 inches in Golden Valley.
Up to 7 inches of rain fell across western Stearns County. Rainfall totals ranged from west to east from 6.50 inches in Brooten to 2.84 inches at St. Cloud State University.
Large hail covered the ground.

Law enforcement officials also reported water was one to two feet deep in some of the streets in Caledonia, La Crescent and Spring Grove.

trained spotter reported dime sized hail at the intersection of Highway 52 and Concord.

Tree limbs were blown down causing intermittent power outages.
Lightning struck several telephone poles and a home, causing a fire.

Large hail severely damaged soybean crops. Corn crops were also damaged, although less yield loss was expected due to the maturity of the corn. The hail covered the ground in many areas, with some drifts up to a foot remaining 10 hours after the end of the storm. The amount of crop damage could not be determined until after harvest. In addition to the crop damage, the hail also damaged vehicles, and the siding and windows on at least one home.
Three inch diameter tree branches were downed.
A number of trees were downed, including one which fell onto a city dock at the swimming beach. The time was estimated.
Large trees were downed.
The top of a machine shed was destroyed.
Large trees were downed.

An old storage barn was destroyed along 375th Avenue near Highway 22 in Section 36 of Lake Prairie West Township. Numerous trees on the same property were felled.  One tree fell onto a swing set and another damaged patio furniture.

A 70x80 foot heavy equipment maintenance shop was demolished along 233rd Street in Platte Township. Sections of the steel roof were found lodged in a ditch and trees along State Highway 25, one quarter mile east of the site. A 34x96 foot hip-roof barn on the same property collapsed and a collection of antique tractors inside was damaged. Also, a storage shed chimney was damaged, a second metal storage shed had one wall blown in 6 inches, and a house sustained damage to a TV tower and shingles. In addition, several trees and at least 2 power poles were downed within a mile vicinity of the farm site.
A two foot diameter Oak tree was downed.
Trees were downed at the south end of Lake Mille Lacs west of Wahkon.
Numerous trees were downed at Twin Bays.
A tree was downed.
Numerous trees were blown down.  A barn and tin shed were blown down.
Numerous trees were blown down.
Hundreds of trees were blown down across Crow Wing County. Nine thousand households lost power.
Trees and power lines were blown down.
Strong winds snapped trees and downed power lines.
Many 4 inch diameter tree branches were blown down.  2.46 inches of rain was also reported.
Large tree branches were blown down.
Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including at least two trees blown down.
Strong winds toppled trees and power lines.  One tree fell on a truck.  A pickup camper was also tipped over.
13 consecutive power poles were snapped.
Large 6 to 8 inch diameter tree limbs were blown down.
Many large trees were knocked down.

The strong winds damaged numerous trees in town and knocked out electricity.  A city sign was damaged and light poles at the fairgrounds were knocked over.  Vendors at the Grant County fairgrounds in Herman had their tents blown over and their contents scattered about.  A rodeo scheduled at the fair was cancelled.
A pole barn sustained considerable damage.

Many county and township roads were closed due to high water levels.  At the intersection of county roads 6 and 1, water was 4 feet deep.  County road 6 had significant overland flows along its entire length from Humboldt to Orleans to Lancaster.  The Lancaster Golf Course was shut down.  Most of Clow, Richardville, and St. Joseph townships were inaccessible due to water on the roads.  U.S. Highway 59 was closed near the Canadian border.  Unofficial rainfall amounts ranged from 11 inches 1.5 miles northwest of Orleans, to 8 inches 5 miles north-northwest of Lancaster, to 7 inches 7 miles north of Lancaster, to 5.8 inches 4 miles west of Orleans, and 4.5 inches at Humboldt.
Most of the hail was dime to quarter size.
The hail was reported by a Sheriff's Deputy 6 miles north and 7 miles west of Karlstad.  Dime to golfball sized hail fell.
The largest hailstones fell a few minutes after the rain had passed.  The large hail dented a vehicle and damaged shingles.
The hail hit the side of a house.
The tornado tracked from 5 miles northwest of Haug to 5 miles north-northwest of Haug.  Many large trees were snapped off or uprooted in a wildlife area.
Dime to nickel-sized hail fell at Oak Island Resort.
The tornado tracked mainly over open fields.
Several county roads were washed out.  Four homes were flooded in Humboldt, Orleans, and Richardson Township.  One of the homes had first floor damage and the other three had basement water damage.
Intermittent touchdowns occurred along the damage path.  Anticyclonic (clockwise) rotation was evident in the tree damage that occurred in shelterbelts.
Dime to golfball sized hail fell.
Penny to nickel sized hail fell.
The hail fell at Little Oak Island.
Pea sized hail covered the ground, mixed with a few hailstones up to a nickel size.

The hail report was based on the continuation of the storm that produced nickel sized hail in northwest Beltrami county near Fourtown.  Public reports were relayed to the National Weather Service that the storm continued into a largely uninhabited area of extreme southern Lake of the Woods county.  The Mayville radar indicated the storm likely produced large hail along the southern edge of Lake of the Woods county.
Ditches and creeks to the Middle River overflowed into fields and overtopped several farm access roads.

Excessive runoff from 4 to 6 inches of rain caused the local ditch network to overflow and flood fields and farmyards.  Flash flooding affected several sections across northern portions of Ester and Northland Townships in extreme northwest Polk county.  The heaviest rain centered on the Northland corner area near state highway 220.
Street flooding occurred near Concordia College and U.S. Highway 75.

This tornado moved east-northeast along a 4 mile curved path and damaged several grain bins, uprooted numerous trees, and demolished portions of a barn 4 miles west of Northcote.  Peak winds were estimated at 130 mph.
The tornado demolished 3 large grain bins, tore the doors off a large steel building, and blew down a small radio tower.  Peak winds were estimated at 140 mph.
Trees and branches were blown down around North Turtle Lake.
The large hail damaged homes, buildings, vehicles, and crops.

The Two Rivers River at Hallock rose over its moderate flood stage of 806 feet MSL late in the evening of June 30th, 2005.  The river peaked around 807.3 feet around noon CDT on July 1st, then began falling.  However, a second peak around 807.4 feet MSL occurred around noon CDT on July 4th.  The river then fell below its moderate flood stage around 3 am CDT on July 7th.

The Two Rivers River at Hallock rose above its moderate flood stage of 806 feet MSL around 6 am CDT on July 9th, 2005.  The river crested around 806.3 feet MSL around 1 am CDT on July 10th, then fell below moderate flood stage around 9 pm CDT on July 10th.
Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including several trees blown down. At least one fallen tree blocked a street. Trees falling on power lines caused a power outage.
Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including several trees blown down. One tree fell on and damaged a house. A pipe was impaled into the wall of a house. The winds also destroyed a small shed.
Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including 2 inch diameter branches blown down.
Several 1 to 2 foot diameter trees were blown down.
The hail fell on the northwest edge of Fergus Falls.
The large hail smashed into homes, vehicles, and crops.  One nursery was hit particularly hard, with greenhouses, plants, and a number of vehicles damaged.
A tree blew over onto a power line, knocking out electricity to 300 Lake Alice residents.

A grain bin was torn from its foundation and was blown across a road.  Another grain bin was torn from its moorings.  This bin rolled east and broke power lines next to the railroad tracks.  It then rolled over the tracks, over highway 9, and into the west edge of Norcross.  A train had to be stopped, as the wires laid across the tracks.  Many trees were also blown down.
A persistent wall cloud with pendant funnel was seen during lightning flashes.
Large tree limbs were blown down.
Large trees were blown down in Hallock and the surrounding area.
A brief tornado tore up several trees.
Branches were blown down.
Large branches were blown down.
The gust was measured by a MNDOT RWIS site.
Extensive street flooding occurred in the downtown area.
Streets were flooded in the downtown area of East Grand Forks.
Several fields were flooded in central Tynsid Township, after 4.5 inches of rain fell.
Penny to nickel sized hail fell.
Heavy rain caused flooding of numerous roads and fields, and also caused basement flooding. An unofficial report of 7 inches of rain was received from Steen.
Heavy rain of up to 5 inches caused flooding of basements and some roads.
Tree limbs were blown down.

Numerous county and township roads were impassable due to overland flooding over the northern half of the county.  Water four feet deep flowed over county road 6 near its intersection with county road 1.
Two inches of rain was also measured.

Several farmsteads were surrounded by flooded fields and ditches between the communities of Florian and Middle River.  The Florian village park was flooded by a small creek.  Middle River City Park was flooded by the Middle River.
The hail broke a window on a home.

Hail ranging from three quarters to two inches in diameter was reported by a trained spotter and an off-duty National Weather Service employee.  A deck was dented, a light fixture was broken, some roofs needed repair, and 3 squad cars were damaged in the area.
A total of 3.5 inches of rain caused street flooding.
Lightning struck an empty home.  The resulting fire destroyed a garage and two bedrooms.
Numerous trees and power lines down, including some on vehicles.

An insurance adjuster estimated 400 homes and 75 townhomes were damaged by hail as large as baseballs. Many windows were broken. Numerous businesses had roof and heating/cooling units damaged. See Hennepin County thunderstorm wind report for property damage total.
Hail damaged numerous homes.

Law enforcement reported significant street flooding and water up to the bumpers of cars in parking lots in Excelsior.  Significant portions of Mound were "under water" according to law enforcement.  The county sheriff reported significant flooding in Brooklyn Park.  A holding pond overflowed into 6 homes and minor street flooding was reported in Eden Prairie.  Rainfall totals between the afternoon of the 3rd and morning of the 4th included 5.40 inches in Eden Prairie, 4.20 inches in Minnetonka, 3.85 inches in Wayzata, 2.80 inches at Crystal Airport, and 2.70 inches in Mound.

Three homes were struck by lightning and were damaged by the resulting fire.  The first home on Highview Court suffered damage when lightning struck the outside gas meter.  An outer wall caught fire and damage was estimated at $4,000.  The second house on Prairie Hills Lane was engulfed in flames and has extensive roof and garage damage totaling $500,000.  The third home on Century Lane suffered $100,000 in fire damage to interior walls after two bolts struck the building.

Heavy rain of four to five inches fell in less than two hours in some locales.  Flooding was reported in Blaine at the intersection of Highway 65 and Main Street.  The intersection of University Avenue and 73rd in Fridley was impassable due to high water, and street flooding was reported elsewhere in Fridley.  Street flooding was also reported in Coon Rapids and Spring Lake Park.  The city manager in Coon Rapids measured 4.25 inches.

Four to five inches of rain fell in less than two hours, causing flooding of numerous streets and intersections.  High water resulted in impassable conditions at Highway 96 and Lexington in Shoreview.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A home was hit by lightning and burned to the ground, but no injuries were reported.

Strong winds accompanied low pressure as it moved across the Upper Mississippi Valley, with a 60 mph wind gust recorded at Canton.  Gusts of 45 to 55 mph were common elsewhere around southeast Minnesota, as well as adjacent areas of northeast Iowa and southwest Wisconsin.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hangars were damaged at the city airport. About 100 trees, some greater than 1 foot in diameter, were downed onto houses, power lines, yards, and cars across town. A gazebo on First Street NE was also destroyed.   Many nature paths at River Bend Nature Center were blocked by downed trees.
Dime sized hail was reported south of Highway 62 one mile east of Dell Road, in addition to the intersection of Highway 5 and Eden Prairie Drive.
Trees were downed onto a home just west of Summit Lake on 1st Avenue E.  Scattered trees were also downed in the northeast portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system over North Central Iowa during the morning of the 28th, moved to South Central Minnesota by the late afternoon, and then marched to a position over Western Lake Superior by the morning of the 29th. Aided by sustained northerly winds of 26 to 37 knots (30 to 43 mph), with gusts to 47 knots (54 mph), the snow and blowing snow reduced visibilities to near zero across much of Chippewa, Lac Qui Parle, Stevens, Swift and Yellow Medicine Counties.  Snow accumulations were very difficult to measure considering the high winds, with most measurements in the 3 to 6 inch range reported across the region.  At or around 07:00 CST on the 28th, ice buildup from a period of freezing rain disabled four substations owned by Minnesota Valley Cooperative Light and Power, located near the South Dakota border in Lac Qui Parle County.  Power lines were also severed across portions of Chippewa, Lac Qui Parle and Yellow Medicine Counties during the morning hours after sunrise. Portions of Lac Qui Parle Power (including the city of Madison), and parts of Yellow Medicine County were without power for as little as one hour, and for as long as 24 hours. Thousands of customers were affected.  The longest duration of power disruption occurred near the Minnesota/South Dakota border. The Blizzard was also responsible for closing portions of Interstate 94, U.S. 10 and Minnesota 210 during the overnight on the 28th. Numerous automobile and truck accidents were reported across the region. Information on power outgages and road closures provided by Montevideo American News and Owatonna People's Press.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two destructive storms swept across Anoka County, knocking over tens of thousands of trees, blowing roofs off houses, and producing large hail and two tornadoes.  See separate reports for the hail and tornadoes.  One storm moved across northwestern and central Anoka County, and the other tracked across southern Anoka County. The property damage report of $85 Million dollars covers all damage from wind and hail in Anoka County on 9-21-05.  Many cities declared a state of emergency.The first storm knocked down three foot diameter trees in Ramsey and tipped over vehicles at an RV dealership.  In Andover, several homes had roofs completely blown off, where the strongest downburst winds were estimated to have reached 85 knots. Numerous garages and sheds were blown apart.  A portion of a gym roof was torn off at a school.  About 10% of roads were blocked by fallen trees.  The storm also moved across Coon Rapids, where 500 trees were felled at Bunker Hills Golf Course, the roof was blown off an office building, a fourplex had its roof partially blown off, and the Riverview neighborhood was "smothered" in downed trees.  This storm was accompanied by two tornadoes, one in Andover and another tracking from Coon Rapids to Blaine (see separate tornado entries).  The F0 tornado in Andover produced very little damage, but was accompanied by a very strong rear flank downdraft, which did the vast majority of the damage in Andover.The second storm tracked across southern Anoka County, affecting Fridley the most.  This was the storm that produced significant damage over Hennepin County, and later on, Ramsey County.  In Fridley, several thousands of trees were toppled, causing structural damage to about 400 homes.  Many roads were blocked and about 90% of the city lost power.  Three people were injured when clearing fallen trees.  A damage survey revealed both inflow and downburst damage as the mesocyclone tracked over Fridley.  Spring Lake Park and Columbia Heights also saw hundreds of downed trees, some on houses and across roads.

A large storm swept across most of northern Hennepin County, accompanied by large hail and a brief tornado.  An NWS damage survey, spotter reports, video, and radar imagery indicate the storm had a very strong rear flank downdraft which was reported to have been sustained as long as 10 minutes in some locations. The wind and hail were responsible for virtually all damage (see separate reports for the hail), and a tornado was on the ground only briefly in Brooklyn Park (see separate tornado entry for Hennepin County).  Tens of thousands of trees were downed, including some snapped a few feet off the ground.  Many roads were blocked. Some neighborhoods were without power for more than one week, as power crews had a massive number of repairs to make.  At the height of the storm, XCEL Energy estimated over 200,000 customers in the western half of the Twin Cities metropolitan area were without power.  many cities declared a state of emergency.  The property damage report of 130 Million dollars covers all wind and hail reports from this storm in Hennepin County on 9-21-05.In Rogers, part of an apartment complex roof ripped off, and a roof from a nearby home was torn off and blown into another home, where it pushed a wall in.  In Hassan Township, numerous trees were toppled, including some on houses, causing major damage. A garage was torn away from a house, and a construction trailer was destroyed. In Dayton, a barn was destroyed and numerous trees felled.  Champlin also had many trees downed.  A roof was blown off an apartment building in Osseo, and numerous trees were down on homes and businesses.  In Maple Grove, many trees were toppled and windows blown out.  A small transmission line went down along with 100 feeder lines.  New Hope and Golden Valley also had downed trees.  Many trees in Robbinsdale and Crystal were also toppled, especially in the north and northeast parts of Robbinsdale and Crystal. Several hangars at the Crystal airport had metal siding and roofs off.  A gust of 68 mph was measured at the Crystal airport at 1813 CST.  Hundreds of trees were felled in Brooklyn Center, and tile from the roofs of a hotel and restaurant were blown off.Perhaps the most severe damage occurred in Brooklyn Park.  City officials estimated at least 10,000 trees were downed, and a local nursery owner estimated 20,000 trees.  Hundreds of trees landed on houses and businesses.  The roof was partially torn off Signature Design Center.  Over 90% of the city lost power.In Minneapolis, most trees were felled in the northern part of the city, namely the Camden, Shingle Creek, Lind-Bohanon, and Victory neighborhoods.  A 45 year old man in the north part of Minneapolis died after getting out of his car.  He was heading for shelter when a large branch landed on him.
An RV dealershp saw all 140 vehicles damaged by hail, including 12 that were totaled.  Hail larger than golf balls went through grills and dented radiators.
Dime sized hail covered the ground at the National Weather Service office.
Power lines were downed across the intersection of West Shadow Lake Drive and Birch Street.

Law enforcement reported water up to the roofs of cars in a low spot in Shakopee.  Rainfall totals between the afternoon of the 3rd and morning of the 4th included 6.62 inches in New Market, 6.17 inches in Cedar Lake Township, 4.76 inches in Shakopee, and 2.6 inches in Prior Lake.
A trained spotter reported nickel to quarter sized hail covering a deck.
A trained spotter reported one to two inch diameter hail.

Torrential rain quickly flooded roads and intersections.  Water was up to car windows at an intersection in Maple Grove, and one road in Brooklyn Park flooded to a depth of five feet.  Single Creek in Brooklyn Park was reported out of its banks.  High water caused stalled cars in Brooklyn Center, near Brookdale Shopping Center.  There was an unofficial report of eight inches rain in Rogers, and street flooding was reported in Champlin.
Quarter sized hail was reported in Crow River via HAM radio.
Quarter sized hail was reported at the intersection of Highways 7 and 22.
Four inch diameter tree branches were downed onto Theodore Worth Parkway near Interstate 394.
Quarter sized hail was reported via HAM radio.
Dime sized hail covered the ground.  Moderate crop damage was reported in the area.
Quarter sized hail fell in Stockholm Township.
Reported by Twin Lakes U.S. Post office employee.
Power lines down. Ten to twelve foot trees down, trees in power lines.
Several streets flooded Chaska, where a few roads had waist deep water.  One automobile stalled due to the flooding.  The NWS office in Chanhassen recorded 4.61 inches rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inverted trough stretched into the Red River Valley, from a low pressure system passing through the central plains.  The precipitation began as a mixture of rain and freezing rain, falling quite heavily at times.  The most freezing rain fell across southeast North Dakota and portions of west central Minnesota, although lighter amounts did fall further north.  Trees and powerlines became weighted down with up to an inch of ice in places.  As temperatures cooled and wind speeds increased, powerlines started to gallop and break in great numbers.  Richland County in North Dakota and Wilkin County in Minnesota were hit the hardest from the freezing rain and power outages.  Thousands of people lost power as several thousand wooden power poles were snapped.  Additionally, one high voltage transmission line in southeast North Dakota was broken.  Roads were blocked by fallen trees, branches, and powerlines.  Many vehicle accidents and several injuries were reported due to the treacherous road conditions.  The wind speeds increased around the noon hour on monday (11-28-05) causing blizzard conditions across southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota.  Whiteout conditions continued into tuesday morning (11-29-05).  Many schools were closed on both monday and tuesday, especially along and south of Interstate 94 in North Dakota and U.S. Highway 10 in Minnesota.  For many schools, these were the first back-to-back day closures since the record winter of 1996-97.  Interstate 29 was closed from Fargo to the South Dakota border, Interstate 94 was closed from Jamestown to Fergus Falls, and U.S. Highway 10 was closed from Moorhead to Detroit Lakes.  Wilkin County reported 70 power poles down in one four mile stretch.  Clay, Norman, and Wilkin Counties received a Presidential Disaster Declaration.  The Governor of Minnesota sent National Guard troops and generators to Wolverton in northern Wilkin County.

A surface low pressure system across Southeast South Dakota late on the evening of the 29th, moved to southern Minnesota by late on the morning of the 30th. It then marched into West Central Wisconsin during the evening hours of the 30th. A large swath of snowfall in the 6 to 8 inch range,  fell approximately north of a line from Madison to Redwood Falls, to Glencoe, to Chanhassen, to Andover, to Woodbury.  Some of the heaviest snowfall occurred West of a Granite Falls to Willmar line, where reports of between 8 and 11 inches were recorded.  In Willmar, several vehicles were reported in the ditch, a semi-truck rolled onto its side, along with minor automotive crashes during the course of the storm, as reported by the Wilmar West Central Tribune.

A storm system located over Eastern North Dakota and Northwestern Minnesota during the lateevening hours on the 13th, moved over Central Minnesota by the afternoon on the 14th, and exitedinto West Central Wisconsin by the early morning hours on the 15th.  Much of the 6 to 8 inch snowfalloccurred roughly East of a line from Foley to Winsted, to Henderson, to Mankato, to Winnebago, andsouth of a line approximately from Foley to Rush City. The largest storm total was 9.2 inches reported at Winsted in Mcleod County.

Precipitation began as freezing rain on the afternoon and evening of the 28th across north Saint Louis, Koochiching, north Itasca, and Cass Counties. Ice accumulations ranged from a quarter to half an inch shutting down schools and the International Falls Airport. Precipitation changed over to snow on Tuesday morning with accumulations ranging from 3 to 6 inches by noon. Schools were closed again on Tuesday in International Falls and Cook.

Snow began overnight of the 13th into the 14th and ended mid morning on the 15th. Snowfall amounts ranged from 10 to 13 inches in far inland areas of the Minnesota Arrowhead and eastern Iron Range to 23 and 26 inches along the higher terrain of the north shore of Lake Superior in places such as Finland and Silver Bay. Other amounts included 12" at Lutsen, 10.5" at Little Marais, close to the lake, 26.5" 7 miles north of Two Harbors, and 15.8" at the Duluth NWS. On December 14th a new daily snowfall record of 13.2 inches was set at Duluth. Schools were closed on the 14th in Duluth and all along the north shore.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two people sustained minor injuries when lightning struck a nearby tree.
Dime to quarter sized hail fell.
Low lying intersections in town were covered by water.
Trees were downed in Hamburg.  Trees and a power line were downed in Norwood-Young America.
Golf ball to baseball sized hail fell in and just east of Boyd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of southeast Minnesota was affected by heavy snow, with amounts of 4 to 8 inches common.  Highest storm total accumulations as reported by volunteer weather observers included 8.0 inches at KAAL-TV in Austin (Mower County), 7.5 inches at Claremont (Dodge County) and Highland (Fillmore County), 7.1 inches at the Rochester International Airport (Olmsted County) and 7.0 inches at Grand Meadow and Racine (Mower County).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail covered the ground at the National Weather Service office.
A pilot reported quarter sized hail in a PIREP from Minneapolis Crystal Airport.
A total of 1.35 inches of rain fell in 35 minutes.  Minor street flooding was reported in poor drainage areas.
Up to 4 inches of water was running across Robert Street between Annapolis Street and Butler Street.
A car was submerged in flood waters.

Law enforcement reported significant flooding at several city intersections.  A bluff slump was reported along Highway 101, but did not impact the roadway.  Rainfall totals between the afternoon of the 3rd and morning of the 4th included 5.66 inches 2 miles southwest of Chanhassen, 5.55 inches at the National Weather Service office in Chanhassen, and 3.12 inches in Chaska.
A shed was damaged. Trees were downed along Arizona Street NW.
Two boats on area lakes were flipped and lost.
Trees up to 3 feet in diameter were downed.
Hail estimated around two inches in diameter was pictured in a local newspaper.
A lightning strike started a fire that cauased $500,000 damage to a home in Minnetrista.  Lightning related house fires were also reported in Eden Prairie and Mound.
Tree limbs were downed.
A total of 2.5 inches of rain was recorded in 45 minutes 4 miles west of Lakeside.  Field flooding was reported south of Bird Island along Highway 5.

Medium sized trees were downed on 1st Avenue and Sunset. A large tree was also downed onto railroad tracks at the County Road 63 intersection.
